coredata.dc:title: Crown-ether-bridging bis-diphenylacrylonitrile macrocycle: The effective fluorescence sensor for oxytetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yunmei
Zheng, Sining
Ye, Yanying
Guo, Hongyu
Yang, Fafu
coredata.dc:description: This work reported the novel organic fluorescence sensor for oxytetracyline based on a novel AIE-macrocycle. By “1 + 1” condensation of diphenylacrylonitrile bearing OH groups and its derivative with terminal Cl groups, the crown-ether-bridging bis-diphenylacrylonitrile macrocycle (CBP) was prepared in quantitative yield of 38 %. CBP exhibited strong AIE fluorescence at 430−550 nm in aqueous media. It presented good selective sensing ability for oxytetracyline with dramatically fluorescence quenching among all kinds of ions and biomolecules. The detection limit was as low as 1.33 × 10−7 M. The sensing mechanism was confirmed as 1:1 stoichiometric ratio. This detection ability was successfully applied on sensing oxytetracycline in test strip and biological samples. Moreover, CBP displayed good sensing ability for oxytetracycline in living cells based on its good living-cell fluorescence imaging property. This paper not only provided the novel organic fluorescence sensor for oxytetracyline, but also opened a new strategy based on AIE-macrocycle to construct the novel sensor for complex biomolecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diphenylacrylonitrile
Macrocycle
Sensor
Fluorescence
Oxytetracycline

pH influence and interference experiments on sensing OTC
Sensing OTC in water-based biological samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (upper) Fluorescence emission spectrum of CBP in THF/H2O solutions with different water contents (1 × 10−6 M, λex =380 nm), (lower) The changes of I/I0 for fluorescence intensities versus the contents of H2O fractions in THF/H2O mixtures, Inset: photo of pure THF and THF/H2O (5 : 95) taken by UV lamp (365 nm).

Fluorescence spectra of CBP (λex =380 nm, 1 × 10−6 M) with guests (1 × 10−6 M) in THF-H2O solution (5:95). (1 = Li+, 2 = Na+, 3 = K+, 4 = Mg2+, 5 = Ca2+, 6 = Al3+, 7 = Ba2+, 8 = Fe3+, 9 = Ag+, 10 = Bi3+, 11 = Cd2+, 12 = Zn2+, 13 = Co2+, 14 = Cr3+, 15 = Mn2+, 16 = Ni2+, 17 = Pb2+, 18 = Pd2+, 19 = Cu2+, 20 = Oxytetracycline, 21 = Vanillin, 22 = Quercetin, 23 = Curcumin, 24 = Carotene, 25 = Lycopene, 26 = Caramel, 27 = Zeaxanthin, 28 = leucine, 29 = cysteine, 30 = asprbic acid, 31 = tryptophan, 32 = guanine, 33 = adenine, 34 = cytosine, 35 = thymine, 36 = fructose, 37 = vitamin B1, 38 = starch, 39 = glucose, 40 = sucrose, 41 = vitamin B2, 42 = caffeine, 43 = Folic acid, 44 = None) Inserted images: left = CBP, right = CBP+OTC.

(Upper) Fluorescence spectra of CBP (1.0×10−6 M) with OTC in various concentrations (H2O-THF (5:95) solution, λ ex =380 nm); (Lower) The plot of fluorescence intensity vs. equivalent concentrations of OTC.
The MALDI-TOF-MS spectrum of CBP with excess OTC.
The 1H NMR spectra of CBP, OTC and CBP with OTC (1:1 ratio, 5 mM each, 26 °C).
The proposal sensing model of CBP with OTC.
Photographs of test strips bearing CBP, CBP + OTC or CBP + other guest (1-19 and 21-43 as described in Fig. 2, using the photograph of CBP + folic acid as example).

Confocal fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells treated with CBP and CBP+OTC (1.0 × 10−6 M for CBP and 5.0 × 10−6 M for OTC). (A)-(C) MCF-7 cells with CBP; (D)-(F) MCF-7 cells with CBP+OTC; Left: bright field images, middle: fluorescence images, right images: the merged images of fluorescence and bright field. (λex =405 nm). The same scale bar for all images.
Synthetic route of target molecule CBP.
Analysis of OTC in different samples.

head.ce:title: Crown-ether-bridging bis-diphenylacrylonitrile macrocycle: The effective fluorescence sensor for oxytetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Fujian Normal University, Fuzhou, 350007, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Fujian Normal University, Fuzhou 350007, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, Fuzhou, 350007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, Fuzhou 350007, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials Oriented Chemical Engineering, Fuzhou, 350007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials Oriented Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials Oriented Chemical Engineering, Fuzhou 350007, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Materials, Fujian Normal University, Fuzhou, 350007, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel organic fluorescence sensor for oxytetracyline based on a novel AIE-macrocycle was achieved.

This work reported the novel organic fluorescence sensor for oxytetracyline based on a novel AIE-macrocycle. By “1 + 1” condensation of diphenylacrylonitrile bearing OH groups and its derivative with terminal Cl groups, the crown-ether-bridging bis-diphenylacrylonitrile macrocycle (CBP) was prepared in quantitative yield of 38 %. CBP exhibited strong AIE fluorescence at 430−550 nm in aqueous media. It presented good selective sensing ability for oxytetracyline with dramatically fluorescence quenching among all kinds of ions and biomolecules. The detection limit was as low as 1.33 × 10−7 M. The sensing mechanism was confirmed as 1:1 stoichiometric ratio. This detection ability was successfully applied on sensing oxytetracycline in test strip and biological samples. Moreover, CBP displayed good sensing ability for oxytetracycline in living cells based on its good living-cell fluorescence imaging property. This paper not only provided the novel organic fluorescence sensor for oxytetracyline, but also opened a new strategy based on AIE-macrocycle to construct the novel sensor for complex biomolecules.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that oxytetracycline (OTC) is a broad spectrum antibiotic used extensively in animal husbandry for the treatment of diseases, health maintenance, and in some countries, at subtherapeutic levels as feed additive to suppress undesirable bacterial infection and promote the growth of livestock, etc [1,2]. However, the irrational use of OTC has resulted in the accumulation of this antibiotic in the human body and animal products such as meat and milk [3,4]. It is illegal to use OTC in honey in China according to the regulation of GB 14963−2011. The maximum allowable OTC content in foods has been limited to 0.1 mg L−1 by WHO [5,6]. Therefore, there is a need to develop the simple, highly sensitive and low cost method for the detection of OTC.

Up to now, some conventional methods, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7,8], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [9], colorimetric analysis [10], and LC with tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS) [11,12], were applied for detecting OTC. Although these methods had their own merits, the disadvantages of each method include the requirement of professional skill and tedious operating procedure for HPLC/LC–MS and less sensitivity and selectivity for colorimetric analysis, all of which limit their practical applications.

Recently, fluorescence sensor was paid much attention in analytical fields, which was regarded as one of the best detect methods due to their merits of high sensitivity, rapidity, simplicity and low costing [13,14]. As a result, series of fluorescence carbon dots and organic-inorganic hybrid nanomaterials had been successfully prepared for detecting OTC with high sensitivity and simple operation process [15–24]. On the other hand, although organic fluorescence sensor possessed the more definite and stable structure and higher selectivity as compared with fluorescence carbon dots and organic-inorganic hybrid nanomaterials, there was only one organic fluorescent sensor based on BODIPY derivative for sensing OTC up to date [25]. The reason might be ascribed to the aggregation caused quench (ACQ) effect of normal organic fluorescence sensor in aqueous solution and the difficulty in designing and synthesizing the suitable host for recognizing the complicated fluorescent guests such as OTC.

Lately, the aggregation induced emission (AIE), as the opposite fluorescence phenomenon of ACQ, opened a new strategy for constructing novel organic fluorescence sensor in aqueous media [26–29]. Series of AIE sensors were also reported for detecting ions and biomolecules with excellent sensitivity and selectivity [30–46]. Nevertheless, the accurate design and synthesis of AIE sensor for complicated fluorescent guests such as OTC is still a challenging work. Generally, the construction of macrocyclic structure, such as macrocyclic crown ether, cucurbit[n]uril and pillararenes [47–51], is an effective strategy to realize the selective recognition for complicated fluorescent guests. But this strategy was not applied for preparing AIE diphenylacrylonitrile-macrocyclic sensor so far. In this work, we wish to report the design and synthesis of the first diphenylacrylonitrile macrocycle bridged by two crown ether chains (named as CBP in Scheme 1 ). Moreover, it exhibited outstanding selective sensing ability for OTC in water-based biological samples and living-cell imaging.

All chemicals including organic and inorganic reagents were obtained from Tansuo Chemicals Co., Ltd and Aladdin Co., Ltd. Acetonitrile and dichloromethane (DCM) were treated by molecular sieve (5 Å) to remove water. Metal nitrates were used to test the sensing abilities for metal ions. The reaction progress was monitored by silica gel on thin layer chromatography (TLC) plates (GF254). The silica gel (200–300 mesh) was applied to the column chromatography for the purification of compounds. The 1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on the Bruker-ARX 400 instrument (Bruker, Germany). The MS data were obtained on the Bruker mass spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with 1,8,9-anthracenetriol as matrix. The UV–vis spectral experiments were done on Varian UV–vis spectrometer (Varian, USA). Fluorescence spectra were collected on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence absolute yields (ΦF ) were measured on FLS920 Fluorescence Spectrometer equipping with a 6-inch integrating sphere (Edinburgh, England). The living-cell fluorescence imaging was accomplished by using a confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss LSM 710, Jena, Germany). Compound 1 was synthesized according to a procedure previously reported [52].

The mixture of compound 1 (0.364 g, 1 mmol), chloroethanol (0.40 g, 5 mmol), K2CO3 (0.970 g, 7 mmol) and KI (0.166 g, 1 mmol) were stirred and refluxed in 40 mL of dry acetonitrile for 24 h under N2 atmosphere. After reaction, the mixture was cooled, treated with 50 mL of HCl (1 M) and 80 mL of CHCl3. The CHCl3 layer was partitioned and the solvent was evaporated to dryness under reduced pressure. Then the residue was treated by EtOH to afford compound 2 as yellow precipitate in yield of 60 %. Due to the poor solubility, compound 2 was not characterized and used for next step directly.

Under the protection of N2 atmosphere, compound 2 (0.452 g, 1 mmol) and chloroacetyl chloride (2 mL) was stirred and refluxed in dry dichloromethane (DCM) (30 mL) for 10 h. The TLC detection suggested the accomplishment of reaction. Then, the mixture was cooled and 30 mL of H2O was added in it. The organic layer was separated and concentrated under reduced pressure. The obtained residue was further purified by recrystallization with CH3OH/DCM (3:1). Compound 3 were obtained as pale yellow solid in yield 85 %. Compound 3: 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ ppm : 7.99 (s, 4H, ArH), 7.68 (d, J =8.8 Hz, 4H, ArH), 7.47 (s, 2H, CH), 7.02 (d, J =8.8 Hz, 4H, ArH), 4.61 (t, 4H, J =6.0 Hz, OCH2), 4.29 (t, 4H, J =6.0 Hz, OCH2), 4.16 (s, 4H, ClCH2). 13C NMR (101 MHz, CDCl3) δ ppm: 165.98, 160.41, 138.88, 129.97, 128.86, 127.43, 1117.79, 115.06, 113.61, 68.30, 65.35, 64.77. MALDI-TOF-MS (C32H26Cl2N2O6) [M]+: calcd for m/z = 604.116; found: m/z = 643.128 (MK+).

The mixture of compound 3 (0.302 g, 0.5 mmol), compound 1 (0.182 g, 0.5 mmol), K2CO3 (0.276 g, 2 mmol) and KI (0.017 g, 0.1 mmol) were stirred and refluxed in 160 mL of dry acetonitrile for 48 h under N2 atmosphere. The reaction was monitored by TLC technique. After reaction, the mixture was treated by 50 mL of HCl (1 M) and extracted with 50 mL of CHCl3. The organic layer was partitioned and concentrated under reduced pressure. The residue was then purified by rapid column chromatography with DCM/ethyl acetate (5:1, V/V) as eluent. The target compound CBP was collected as yellow solid in quantitative yield of 38 %. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d6) δ ppm : 8.04 (s, 8H, ArH), 7.98 (s, 4H, CH), 7.74 (d, J =8.8 Hz, 8H, ArH), 7.11 (d, J =8.8 Hz, 8H, ArH), 4.93 (bs, 4H, OCH2), 4.07 (t, J =6.0 Hz, 8H, OCH2). 13C NMR (101 MHz, DMSO-d6) δ ppm: 174.72, 160.27, 139.65, 135.98, 130.52, 129.76, 127.76, 118.32, 115.51, 111.42, 70.17, 62.12, 59.92. MALDI-TOF-MS (C56H40N4O8) calcd. for m/z = 896.2846; found: m/z = 897.7914(MH+), 913.9931(M+H2O)+, 943.0113 (M + EtOH)+. Anal.calcd for C56H40N4O8: C 74.99, H 4.50, N 6.25; found C 74.93, H 4.55, N 6.19.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The tested procedure of the test paper
ce:section.ce:para: The CBP tetrahydrofuran (THF) solution (0.1 mM) was prepared and the neutral filter paper was soaked in it for 3 min. The filter paper was then dried at room temperature and cut into test strips. Subsequently, 3 drops of 0.1 mM aqueous solution with all kinds of guests were added onto test strips. After drying, ultraviolet (365 nm) was used to obtain the fluorescence photograph of the test strips.

MTT(methylthiazolyldiphenyl tetrazolium) method was used to detect the toxicity of CBP in MCF-7 cancer cells. The inoculated MCF-7 cancer cells were cultured in 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h, followed by CBP solution, washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer, and then cultured in 0.5 mg/mL of MTT-PBS buffer for 3 h. Finally, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (100 μL) was added to dissolve the generated Formazan crystals and the absorption intensity was collected at 490 nm.

3.0 mg of CBP was dissolved in DMSO (1.0 mL) and further diluted to 1.0 × 10−6 M with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). Under the same MTT condition, MCF-7 cancer cells were cultured for 24 h, then stained with CBP (1.0 × 10−6 M) for 24 h. After washing with PBS buffer, CBP staining cells were cultured in OTC solution (5.0 × 10−6 M) at 37 °C for 1 h. CBP and CBP + OTC staining cells were observed under confocal laser scanning microscope.

In order to construct diphenylacrylonitrile macrocycle which was expected to recognize the large and complicated guest OTC, two crown ether chains were designed as spacers to connect two bis-diphenylacrylonitrile units, in which the cavity was enough to accommodate OTC and the oxygen group was favorable for hydrogen-bond action with OTC. As shown in Scheme 1, according to the published procedure [52], by reacting p-hydroxyphenylacetonitrile and terephthalaldehyde, diphenylacrylonitrile derivative 1 was synthesized in yield of 78 % under the reaction condition of NaOH/EtOH. Then, by reacting compound 1 with chloroethanol in K2CO3/MeCN system using KI as catalyst, compound 2 was obtained in yield of 60 %. Further refluxing compound 2 with chloroacetyl chloride in CH2Cl2 afforded compound 3 bearing terminal Cl groups in yield of 85 %. Finally, by the “1 + 1” condensation based on etherification reaction of compounds 1 and 3 under diluted condition, the target compound CBP was prepared in quantitative yield of 38 % after purification of column chromatography. The structure of target compound CBP was confirmed by 1H and 13C NMR spectra, MALDI-TOF-MS spectra and elemental analysis (see SI). The strong peak for MH+ indicated the fulfilment of “1 + 1” condensation. The peaks for (M+H2O)+ and (M + EtOH)+ might suggest the excellent inclusion ability of CBP for guests. The 1H and 13C NMR spectra agreed well with the structure. The inclusion of EtOH was also confirmed by the 1H NMR (Fig. S4). The elemental contents of elemental analysis also were in accordance with the structure of CBP.

Firstly, the AIE property of CBP was explored in the normal tested system of THF-H2O solution for AIE study. The UV absorption curves of CBP in THF-H2O system displayed the absorption scope at wavelength of 350−450 nm with the changeable absorption intensities (Fig. S7). The fluorescence changes of CBP in THF/H2O system are illustrated in Fig. 1 . It indicated that, with the increase of water contents, the fluorescence emission intensities also increased gradually. The maximum fluorescence appeared in THF-H2O system with f w = 90 %. The corresponding fluorescence intensity increased by 86 times, which was very high among all kinds of AIE derivatives. CBP exhibited dazzling fluorescence in THF/H2O (1:9) solution as shown in Fig. 1 (inserted picture). The little decrease of fluorescence intensity in THF/H2O (5:95) could be ascribed to the low solubility of CBP in aqueous solution. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield was as high as 0.78 and 0.75 in THF/H2O (1:9) and THF/H2O (5:95), respectively. Moreover, it can be seen that maximum fluorescence wavelength shifted from 489 nm to 474 nm, suggesting the H-aggregation for CBP in THF/H2O solution. These AIE features were in accordance with the structural characteristic of CBP. Due to cyclic structure, the two diphenylacrylonitrile units easily produced the intramolecular rotation limit to afford strong AIE effect. On the other hand, the symmetrical structure of CBP was favourable for the H-aggregation of two diphenylacrylonitrile units. All these AIE studies meant that CBP has outstanding fluorescence emission performance in aqueous media, which contributed to the investigation of guest-sensing behavior in aqueous environments and living bodies.

As CBP displayed the strong fluorescence in THF-H2O solution with high water contents, the solution system of THF-H2O (5:95) was used as aqueous media to examine the sensing ability of CBP for all kinds of metallic ions and biomolecules. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence spectra of CBP with guests had no obvious shift on maximum emission wavelength, but some changes on fluorescence emission intensities. One can see that, after adding various metallic ions and biomolecules (including Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Bi3+, Cd2+ Zn2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Pd2+, Cu2+, vanillin, quercetin, curcumin, carotene, lycopene, caramel, zeaxanthin, leucine, cysteine, asprbic acid, tryptophan, guanine, adenine, cytosine, thymine, fructose, vitamin B1, starch, glucose, sucrose, vitamin B2, caffeine and folic acid), the fluorescence intensities of CBP showed small changes. The fluctuation percentage of fluorescence intensities were less than 15 %. However, when OTC was added in CBP solution, the fluorescence was quenched dramatically with an 88 % decrease of fluorescence intensity. These phenomena suggested the good selective sensing ability of CBP for OTC with “turn-off” fluorescence behavior among all tested species. This kind of fluorescence sensor for OTC was seldom observed among various organic fluorescence sensors and AIE sensors [25].

As CBP has good selective sensing abilities for OTC, the fluorescence titration of CBP for OTC were measured in H2O-THF system (95 % of H2O). As displayed in Fig. 3 , with the increase of the concentrations of OTC, the fluorescence emission at 430−560 nm decreased dramatically and the fluorescence was quenched gradually from dazzling to faint (Fig. 3 (upper)). Furthermore, the corresponding plot of fluorescence intensity vs. the equivalent concentrations is illustrated in Fig. 3 (lower). According to the formula for calculating detection limit of DL = K × Sb1 / S (where K = 2 or 3 usually, here K was 2, Sb1 means the standard deviation of blank solution and S represent the value of curve slope), the detection limit of CBP for OTC was assessed as 1.33 × 10−7 M, which was less than the maximum allowable OTC content established by the WHO (0.1 mg L-1). This result suggested the good potential application of CBP for sensing OTC in water media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH influence and interference experiments on sensing OTC
ce:section.ce:para: pH adaptability is the important parameter in practical application. Therefore, the fluorescence behavior of CBP and CBP with OTC was examined in the pH range of 3−11. As shown in Fig. S8, CBP possessed the stable fluorescence emission in pH 5–9, although a slight decrease of fluorescence intensities were observed. After adding OTC, the fluorescence of CBP was quenched dramatically and the lowest fluorescence intensity was at pH = 7. The weak fluorescence gradually became stronger at pH > 8 or pH < 6. These phenomena might be ascribed to the transition of OTC from neutral structure to ionic structure under pH > 8 or pH < 6, resulting in the destruction of electronic structure of OTC and then destroying the host-guest interaction. On the other hand, the interference experiments of other species on sensing OTC were investigated and the fluorescence spectra are summarized in Fig. S9. It can be seen that the fluorescence of CBP with OTC + other species were similar with that of CBP with OTC. These results suggested that the selective sensing behavior of CBP for OTC was little influenced by the tested ions and biomolecules, indicating the potential application for detecting OTC in a complex biological environment.

The sensing mechanism of CBP for OTC was studied by MS spectroscopy, fluorescence Job’s plot and 1H NMR spectroscopy. When excess OTC (5 eq.) was added in CBP solution, the MS spectroscopy of this system exhibited a strong binding peak at 1357.9 [(CBP+OTC)+] and no other binding peak appeared, suggesting the stable 1:1 stoichiometric ratio of CBP with OTC (Fig. 4 ). The fluorescence Job’s plot as shown in Fig. S10 displayed the obvious inflection point at f OTC = 0.5. This plot also implied the stoichiometric ratio of 1:1. In Fig. 5 , the 1H NMR spectra of CBP, OTC and CBP+OTC (1:1) were gathered for comparison (The full 1H NMR spectra were shown in Fig. S4 and S11). It can be seen that all proton signals of CBP moved a little from low field to high field. The proton signals of OH and NH of OTC displayed a shift from high field to low field obviously. These changes suggested that both the diphenylacrylonitrile skeleton and the crown ether bridging were involved in binding OTC. The OH and NH groups on OTC produced hydrogen-bonding action with the oxygen and nitrogen groups of CBP. In order to confirm that the OTC was binded in the two conjugated planes of CBP or out the cavity, the complex 1H NMR spectra of OTC and precursor 3 with the similar structure for CBP was investigated. The result suggested that no change in 1H NMR spectra was observed for this system (Fig. S12), indicating no molecular interaction when OTC was beside the bis-diphenylacrylonitrile. On the contrary, the obvious changes were observed when OTC was added in solution of CBP. By comparison with the two different results, it was reasonable to propose the OTC was in the two conjugated planes of CBP. Based on the above binding analysis, the sensing model of CBP for OTC was proposed as shown in Fig. 6 , in which the OTC was bound in the cavity of CBP. The stable hydrogen-bonding action existed between OH and NH groups of OTC and the oxygen and nitrogen groups of CBP.

Test strip was further used to evaluate the practical application on sensing OTC. The test paper bearing CBP was prepared by immersing the neutral filter paper in CBP solution of THF (0.1 mM) for 3 min, which was then dried at room temperature and cut into test strips. Subsequently, 3 drops of 0.1 mM aqueous solution with all kinds of guests were added onto test strips. As shown in Fig. 7 , the test strip without guest showed bright blue cyan color, which still maintained the strong fluorescence after adding the ions (1–19 as described in Fig. 2) or biomolecules (21–43 as described in Fig. 2, using the photograph of CBP + folic acid as example). However, after adding OTC solution, the fluorescence became faint obviously under ultraviolet light (365 nm). These results certainly implied the practical application prospect of CBP on sensing OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing OTC in water-based biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practical application of the novel OTC sensor, standard recovery experiments were conducted to analyze three water-based biological samples: fluid milk, liquid egg-white and urine. All these samples were spiked with OTC at concentrations of 5, 10, 15 μM, which were further directly analyzed by the fluorescent strategy based on the standard curves and regression equations. The results are listed in Table 1 . The recoveries of this fluorescence method fluctuated in the range of 96 %–104 %. Considering the complex nature of fluid milk, egg-white and urine, the good recoveries suggested the broaden application prospect of CBP for detecting OTC in water-based biological samples.

The application of CBP for detecting OTC was studied by living-cell imaging under confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The metabolic activity of CBP for MCF-7 cells was explored by MTT method at the concentration of 0–5.0 × 10−5 M. As illustrated in Fig. S13, the cell activity was higher than 80 % when the concentration of CBP was smaller than 1.0 × 10−5 M, indicating the low biotoxicity of CBP. Moreover, the MCF-7 cells were treated by CBP for 1 h at 37 °C. The fluorescence imaging of living cells was detected at λex =405 nm. The strong turquoise fluorescence was observed as shown in Fig. 8 (A–C). When the cells were treated with CBP and OTC sequentially, the strong fluorescence of cells was quenched dramatically and almost no fluorescence was observed. This result was consistent with the fluorescent titration experiments, suggesting that CBP not only possessed good living-cell fluorescence imaging ability, but also the excellent sensing ability for OTC in living cells. To the best of our knowledge, this work was the novel organic fluorescence probe for sensing OTC in aqueous media and was applied in living-cell imaging, which provided a new strategy for the detection of OTC in water-based biological samples and the living cell with its environment.

In conclusion, this work has focused on design and synthesis of the novel orgainc fluorescence sensor for OTC based on a novel AIE-macrocycle. A symmetric crown-ether-bridging bis-diphenylacrylonitrile macrocycle (CBP) was prepared in quantitative yield of 38 % by “1 + 1” condensation of diphenylacrylonitrile with OH groups and its derivative with terminal Cl groups. It emitted high fluorescence at 430−550 nm in aqueous media due to the strong AIE effect. It exhibited good selective sensing behavior for OTC based on the dramatically decrease of fluorescence. The detection limit was of 1.33 × 10−7 M. The sensing mechanism of 1:1 stoichiometric ratio was studied by MS spectrum, fluorescence Job’s plot, and 1H NMR spectrum. This sensing ability for OTC was successfully used for detecting OTC in test strip and water-based biological samples. CBP also possessed the good fluorescence living-cell imaging property and had good sensing ability for OTC in living cells. In summay, this paper reported the novel orgainc fluorescence sensor for OTC, opening a new path to construct a novel sensor for complex biomolecules in aqueous media based on AIE-macrocycle.
Yunmei Wu: Investigation, Methodology. Sining Zheng: Data curation. Yanying Ye: Formal analysis. Hongyu Guo: Writing - original draft. Fafu Yang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no Conflict of Interest for this paper entitled “Crown-ether-bridging bis-diphenylacrylonitrile macrocycle: The effective fluorescence sensor for oxytetracycline” (Authors: Yunmei Wu, Sining Zheng, Yanying Ye, Hongyu Guo, and Fafu Yang)”, which was submitted to JOURNAL OF PHOTOCHEMISTRY AND PHOTOBIOLOGY A-CHEMISTRY.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No:21406036), Fujian Science and Technology Project (No. 2019N0010) and Undergraduate innovation program of FJNU (2021) were greatly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113219.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: First report on electrocatalytic oxidation of oxytetracycline by horse radish peroxidase: Application in developing a biosensor to oxytetracycline determination
Sens. Actuat. B

J. Vet. Med. A
